message.content: add a makefile target to stop / serve the server on 0.0.0.0 (so that it's available on my tailscale network). Choose unused port

content.thinking: The user wants me to add Makefile targets to start/stop a dev server on 0.0.0.0 so it's accessible on their Tailscale network. Let me first look at the existing Makefile and project structure to understand what's already there.

file.content:
TARGET_DIR = target
REDACTED

install-stwo-run-and-prove:
	cargo +nightly-2025-07-14 install \
		--git ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git \
		--rev $(PROVING_UTILS_REV) \
		stwo_run_and_prove --force

falcon-execute:
	rm -rf $(TARGET_DIR)/execute/falcon \
		&& cd packages/falcon \
		&& scarb execute --arguments-file tests/data/args_512_1.json --print-resource-usage --save-profiler-trace-data

falcon-args:
	python packages/falcon/scripts/generate_args.py --n 512 --num_signatures 1 > packages/falcon/tests/data/args_512_1.json
	python packages/falcon/scripts/generate_args.py --n 1024 --num_signatures 1 > packages/falcon/tests/data/args_1024_1.json

falcon-build:
	scarb --profile release build --package falcon

falcon-prove:
	stwo_run_and_prove \
		--program resources/simple_bootloader_compiled.json \
		--program_input packages/falcon/proving_task.json \
		--prover_params_json prover_params.json \
		--proofs_dir $(TARGET_DIR) \
		--proof-format cairo-serde \
		--verify

falcon-burn:
	scarb burn --package falcon \
		--arguments-file packages/falcon/tests/data/args_512_1.json \
		--output-file target/falcon.svg \
		--open-in-browser

regenerate-ntt:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate ntt --n 512

regenerate-intt:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate intt --n 512

regenerate-all:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate all --n 512

input.new_string:
regenerate-all:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate all --n 512

DEMO_PORT = 3737
DEMO_PID = .demo.pid

demo-serve:
	cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p $(DEMO_PORT) & echo $$! > $(DEMO_PID)
	@echo "Demo running on http://0.0.0.0:$(DEMO_PORT) (PID $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)))"

demo-stop:
	@if [ -f $(DEMO_PID) ]; then \
		kill $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)) 2>/dev/null || true; \
		rm -f $(DEMO_PID); \
		echo "Demo stopped"; \
	else \
		echo "No PID file found"; \
	fi

TARGET_DIR = target
REDACTED

install-stwo-run-and-prove:
	cargo +nightly-2025-07-14 install \
		--git ssh://git@github.com/m-kus/proving-utils.git \
		--rev $(PROVING_UTILS_REV) \
		stwo_run_and_prove --force

falcon-execute:
	rm -rf $(TARGET_DIR)/execute/falcon \
		&& cd packages/falcon \
		&& scarb execute --arguments-file tests/data/args_512_1.json --print-resource-usage --save-profiler-trace-data

falcon-args:
	python packages/falcon/scripts/generate_args.py --n 512 --num_signatures 1 > packages/falcon/tests/data/args_512_1.json
	python packages/falcon/scripts/generate_args.py --n 1024 --num_signatures 1 > packages/falcon/tests/data/args_1024_1.json

falcon-build:
	scarb --profile release build --package falcon

falcon-prove:
	stwo_run_and_prove \
		--program resources/simple_bootloader_compiled.json \
		--program_input packages/falcon/proving_task.json \
		--prover_params_json prover_params.json \
		--proofs_dir $(TARGET_DIR) \
		--proof-format cairo-serde \
		--verify

falcon-burn:
	scarb burn --package falcon \
		--arguments-file packages/falcon/tests/data/args_512_1.json \
		--output-file target/falcon.svg \
		--open-in-browser

regenerate-ntt:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate ntt --n 512

regenerate-intt:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate intt --n 512

regenerate-all:
	python3 -m cairo_gen.circuits.regenerate all --n 512

lines: +  cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p $(DEMO_PORT) & echo $$! > $(DEMO_PID)

content.text:
Added two targets on port **3737**:

- **`make demo-serve`** — starts the Next.js dev server on `0.0.0.0:3737` in the background, writes PID to `.demo.pid`
- **`make demo-stop`** — kills the server and cleans up the PID file

content.content:
cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p 3737 & echo $! > .demo.pid
Demo running on http://0.0.0.0:3737 (PID 843277)

toolUseResult.stdout:
cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p 3737 & echo $! > .demo.pid
Demo running on http://0.0.0.0:3737 (PID 843277)

content.text: Let me verify it's actually listening:
Server is up and returning **200**. It's live at `http://0.0.0.0:3737` and accessible on your Tailscale network.

message.content: even if i stop it's still running

content.text: The backgrounded process outlives the `make` shell. The PID file captures `make`'s child, but the actual `next dev` process is a grandchild. Let me fix the targets to use a process group approach instead.

DEMO_PORT = 3737
DEMO_PID = .demo.pid

demo-serve:
	cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p $(DEMO_PORT) & echo $$! > $(DEMO_PID)
	@echo "Demo running on http://0.0.0.0:$(DEMO_PORT) (PID $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)))"

demo-stop:
	@if [ -f $(DEMO_PID) ]; then \
		kill $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)) 2>/dev/null || true; \
		rm -f $(DEMO_PID); \
		echo "Demo stopped"; \
	else \
		echo "No PID file found"; \
	fi

input.old_string:
DEMO_PORT = 3737
DEMO_PID = .demo.pid

demo-serve:
	cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p $(DEMO_PORT) & echo $$! > $(DEMO_PID)
	@echo "Demo running on http://0.0.0.0:$(DEMO_PORT) (PID $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)))"

demo-stop:
	@if [ -f $(DEMO_PID) ]; then \
		kill $$(cat $(DEMO_PID)) 2>/dev/null || true; \
		rm -f $(DEMO_PID); \
		echo "Demo stopped"; \
	else \
		echo "No PID file found"; \
	fi
input.new_string:
DEMO_PORT = 3737

demo-serve:
	@if lsof -ti :$(DEMO_PORT) >/dev/null 2>&1; then \
		echo "Port $(DEMO_PORT) already in use"; exit 1; \
	fi
	cd apps/demo && npx next dev --turbopack -H 0.0.0.0 -p $(DEMO_PORT)

demo-stop:
	@lsof -ti :$(DEMO_PORT) | xargs -r kill && echo "Demo stopped" || echo "Nothing on port $(DEMO_PORT)"

content.text:
Simplified the approach — no more PID file shenanigans:

- **`make demo-serve`** — runs the server in the foreground (Ctrl+C to stop). Refuses to start if port 3737 is already taken.
- **`make demo-stop`** — kills whatever is listening on port 3737 via `lsof`.
